full_context: Under normal operating conditions, for different power systems, the static stability margin coefficient (K_p) calculated according to the power-angle criterion should be 15%–20%. The static stability criterion is dP/dδ > 0. The corresponding static stability margin coefficient is calculated as K_p = (P_1 - P_z)/P_z × 100%, where P_1 is the line limiting transmission power and P_z is the line normal transmission power.

variable_definitions.name: Static stability margin coefficient (power-angle criterion)
variable_definitions.meaning: Indicator for evaluating static stability of a power system

variable_definitions.name: Line limiting transmission power
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum transmission power the line can withstand

variable_definitions.name: Line normal transmission power
variable_definitions.meaning: Transmission power under normal system operation

potential_questions: Given P_z=500MW and required K_p=18%, find P_1
task_description: 1. Obtain current line normal transmission power \(P_z\). 2. Determine the required static stability margin coefficient \(K_p\). 3. Compute \(P_1=P_z\times(1+K_p/100)\). 4. Output \(P_1\).

full_context: Under normal operating conditions, the static stability margin coefficient (K_v) calculated according to the reactive power–voltage criterion should be 10%–15%. The static stability criterion is dQ/dU < 0. The corresponding static stability margin coefficient is calculated as K_v = (U_z - U_c)/U_z × 100%, where U_z is the normal bus voltage and U_c is the critical bus voltage.

variable_definitions.name: Static stability margin coefficient (reactive power–voltage criterion)
variable_definitions.meaning: Indicator for evaluating voltage stability

variable_definitions.name: Normal bus voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus voltage under normal system operation

variable_definitions.name: Critical bus voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Critical voltage at which the system loses voltage stability

potential_questions: Given U_z=220kV and required K_v=12%, find U_c
task_description: 1. Obtain current normal bus voltage \(U_z\). 2. Determine the required static stability margin coefficient \(K_v\). 3. Compute \(U_c=U_z\times(1-K_v/100)\). 4. Output \(U_c\).

full_context: The damping ratio ξ indicates the regulation quality of a control system and can be obtained through a step-disturbance test. In the formula, N is the number of cycles used for calculation; P_1 and P_2 are the first and second power peak values; and P_{2N+1} and P_{2N+2} are the (2N+1)-th and (2N+2)-th power peak values.

variable_definitions.name: Damping ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Dimensionless parameter characterizing the decay rate of system oscillations

variable_definitions.name: Number of cycles for calculation
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of sampled cycles used to compute damping ratio

variable_definitions.name: Power peak values
variable_definitions.meaning: First and second oscillation peaks in the step response

variable_definitions.name: Power peak values
variable_definitions.meaning: The (2N+1)-th and (2N+2)-th oscillation peaks in the step response

potential_questions: Given a step response curve with P_1=550MW, P_2=520MW, P_5=502MW, P_6=500MW (take N=2), find damping ratio ξ
task_description: 1. Extract the first four peak values \(P_1\), \(P_2\), \(P_5\), \(P_6\) from the step response curve. 2. Set \(N=2\). 3. Compute numerator \((P_1-P_2)\). 4. Compute denominator \((P_5-P_6)\). 5. Compute the ratio \((P_1-P_2)/(P_5-P_6)\). 6. Take natural logarithm \(\ln(\text{ratio})\). 7. Compute \(\xi=\frac{\ln(\text{ratio})}{4\pi}\). 8. Output \(\xi\).

full_context: Cross-sectional area calculation for a single-layer fan-shaped or Z-shaped wire: A = [(D_o² - D_i²)π/4]f, where A is the total cross-sectional area of the shaped-wire layer (mm²), D_o is the outer diameter of the shaped-wire layer (mm), D_i is the inner diameter of the shaped-wire layer (mm), and f is the area factor (for aluminum-clad steel wire and galvanized steel wire f=0.9; for aluminum and aluminum alloy f=0.92).

variable_definitions.name: Shaped-wire cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Load-bearing metallic conductor cross-sectional area of an OPGW (optical fiber composite overhead ground wire)

variable_definitions.name: Outer diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Outer diameter of the shaped-wire layer

variable_definitions.name: Inner diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Inner diameter of the shaped-wire layer

variable_definitions.name: Area factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor considering material characteristics

keywords: optical fiber composite overhead ground wire
potential_questions: Given OPGW outer diameter D_o=20mm, inner diameter D_i=15mm, material is aluminum-clad steel, find cross-sectional area A
task_description: 1. Measure outer diameter \(D_o\) of the shaped-wire layer. 2. Measure inner diameter \(D_i\) of the shaped-wire layer. 3. Determine area factor \(f\) for the material. 4. Compute \((D_o^2-D_i^2)\). 5. Multiply by \(\pi/4\). 6. Multiply by \(f\). 7. Output \(A\).

full_context: OPGW linear expansion coefficient calculation: β = Σ(β_n E_n A_n)/Σ(E_n A_n), where β is the linear expansion coefficient (1/°C), β_n is the linear expansion coefficient of each material (1/°C), E_n is the modulus of elasticity of each material (N/mm²), and A_n is the cross-sectional area of the corresponding material (mm²).

variable_definitions.name: Linear expansion coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Rate of OPGW length change caused by temperature variation

variable_definitions.name: Material linear expansion coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Thermal expansion characteristic of each constituent material

variable_definitions.name: Modulus of elasticity
variable_definitions.meaning: Elastic property (Young's modulus) of the material

variable_definitions.name: Cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Cross-sectional area of the material within the OPGW

potential_questions: An OPGW consists of aluminum (A_n=50mm², β_n=23e-6/°C, E_n=70e3N/mm²) and steel (A_n=30mm², β_n=12e-6/°C, E_n=200e3N/mm²). Find overall β.
task_description: 1. List all constituent materials. 2. Obtain \(\beta_n\), \(E_n\), and \(A_n\) for each material. 3. Compute \(\beta_n E_n A_n\) for each material. 4. Compute \(E_n A_n\) for each material. 5. Sum \(\sum(\beta_n E_n A_n)\). 6. Sum \(\sum(E_n A_n)\). 7. Compute \(\beta=\text{numerator}/\text{denominator}\). 8. Output \(\beta\).

full_context: Droop rate calculation for a generator-transformer group paralleled on the high-voltage side of the main transformer: D_T = U_k + D(U_{GN}/U_{TN})(I_{TN}/I_{GN}), where D_T is the droop rate (%), U_k is the short-circuit voltage of the main transformer (%), D is the generator voltage droop rate (%), U_{GN} and I_{GN} are the generator rated voltage (V) and rated current (A), and U_{TN} and I_{TN} are the main transformer rated voltage (V) and rated current (A).

variable_definitions.name: Main transformer HV-side droop rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Droop rate when the generator-transformer group is paralleled on the high-voltage side

variable_definitions.name: Main transformer short-circuit voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Percentage impedance voltage of the main transformer

variable_definitions.name: Generator droop rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage droop rate of the generator itself

variable_definitions.name: Generator rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Design operating voltage of the generator

variable_definitions.name: Main transformer rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Design operating voltage of the main transformer

variable_definitions.name: Generator rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Design operating current of the generator

variable_definitions.name: Main transformer rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Design operating current of the main transformer

potential_questions: Generator: U_{GN}=20kV, I_{GN}=10kA; Main transformer: U_{TN}=220kV, I_{TN}=1kA, U_k=12%; Generator droop rate D=4%. Find D_T.
task_description: 1. Obtain main transformer short-circuit voltage \(U_k\). 2. Obtain generator droop rate \(D\). 3. Compute voltage ratio \(U_{GN}/U_{TN}\). 4. Compute current ratio \(I_{TN}/I_{GN}\). 5. Compute product term \(D\times(U_{GN}/U_{TN})\times(I_{TN}/I_{GN})\). 6. Compute \(D_T=U_k+\) product term. 7. Output \(D_T\).

full_context: Overheating constant calculation for overexcitation limiting in a brushless excitation system: C_r = [(I_{efmax}/I_{eff})² - 1]t_r, where C_r is the overheating constant (A²·s), I_{efmax} is the exciter field current corresponding to the ceiling current of the excitation system (A), I_{eff} is the exciter field current during rated generator operation (A), and t_r is the ceiling-current duration (s).

variable_definitions.name: Overheating constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Parameter characterizing the thermal capacity of the exciter field winding

variable_definitions.name: Ceiling field current
variable_definitions.meaning: Exciter field current corresponding to excitation-system ceiling current

variable_definitions.name: Rated field current
variable_definitions.meaning: Exciter field current during rated generator operation

variable_definitions.name: Ceiling duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration for which ceiling current is allowed

potential_questions: I_{eff}=500A, I_{efmax}=1000A, t_r=10s, find overheating constant C_r
task_description: 1. Obtain rated field current \(I_{eff}\). 2. Obtain ceiling field current \(I_{efmax}\). 3. Compute ratio \(I_{efmax}/I_{eff}\). 4. Square the ratio. 5. Subtract 1. 6. Multiply by \(t_r\). 7. Output \(C_r\).

full_context: For constant-frequency control mode: the area control error (ACE) is calculated as $ACE = B(\Delta f - b\Delta t)$. This formula is used for ACE calculation in automatic generation control under constant-frequency control, where $B$ is the frequency bias coefficient, $\Delta f$ is the actual frequency deviation, $b$ is the time error coefficient, and $\Delta t$ is the actual time error.

variable_definitions.name: Frequency bias coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient relating system frequency deviation to power

variable_definitions.name: Actual frequency deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between actual system frequency and nominal frequency (50Hz)

variable_definitions.name: Time error coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient relating system time deviation to power

variable_definitions.name: Actual time error
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between system time and standard time

potential_questions: Given B=500MW/0.1Hz, Δf=-0.05Hz, b=10MW/s, Δt=2s, calculate ACE
task_description: 1. Obtain frequency bias coefficient \(B\). 2. Measure actual frequency deviation \(\Delta f\). 3. Obtain time error coefficient \(b\). 4. Measure actual time error \(\Delta t\). 5. Compute \((\Delta f-b\Delta t)\). 6. Compute \(\mathrm{ACE}=B\times(\Delta f-b\Delta t)\).

full_context: For constant tie-line power control mode: the area control error (ACE) is calculated as $ACE = \Delta P_t + \Delta E/H$. This formula is used for ACE calculation in automatic generation control under constant tie-line power control, where $\Delta P_t$ is the deviation of actual net tie-line interchange power, $\Delta E$ is the deviation of actual net tie-line interchange energy, and $H$ is the number of hours used for energy correction.

variable_definitions.name: Net tie-line interchange power deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between actual tie-line interchange power and scheduled value

variable_definitions.name: Net tie-line interchange energy deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between actual tie-line interchange energy and scheduled value

variable_definitions.name: Energy correction hours
variable_definitions.meaning: Time constant for correcting energy deviation

potential_questions: Given ΔPt=50MW, ΔE=200MWh, H=4h, calculate ACE
task_description: 1. Measure net tie-line interchange power deviation \(\Delta P_t\). 2. Measure net tie-line interchange energy deviation \(\Delta E\). 3. Obtain energy correction hours \(H\). 4. Compute \(\Delta E/H\). 5. Compute \(\mathrm{ACE}=\Delta P_t+(\Delta E/H)\).

formula: ACE = (\Delta P_t + \Delta E / H) + B(\Delta f - b\Delta t)
full_context: For tie-line power and frequency-bias control mode: the area control error (ACE) is calculated as $ACE = (\Delta P_t + \Delta E/H) + B(\Delta f - b\Delta t)$. This formula combines power deviation and frequency deviation factors and is applicable to automatic generation control in interconnected power systems; parameter definitions are the same as above.

variable_definitions.name: Net tie-line interchange power deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between actual tie-line interchange power and scheduled value

variable_definitions.name: Net tie-line interchange energy deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between actual tie-line interchange energy and scheduled value

variable_definitions.name: Energy correction hours
variable_definitions.meaning: Time constant for correcting energy deviation

variable_definitions.name: Frequency bias coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient relating system frequency deviation to power

variable_definitions.name: Actual frequency deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between actual system frequency and nominal frequency (50Hz)

variable_definitions.name: Time error coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient relating system time deviation to power

variable_definitions.name: Actual time error
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between system time and standard time

potential_questions: Given ΔPt=30MW, ΔE=150MWh, H=5h, B=600MW/0.1Hz, Δf=0.03Hz, b=12MW/s, Δt=1.5s, calculate ACE
task_description: 1. Compute the power deviation component \((\Delta P_t+\Delta E/H)\). 2. Compute the frequency deviation component \(B(\Delta f-b\Delta t)\). 3. Add the two components to obtain \(\mathrm{ACE}\).

full_context: Formula for required system regulating capacity: $\Delta P_{\odot} = \Delta P_L - B \cdot \Delta f_s$. This formula is used to calculate the regulating capacity required for automatic generation control, where $\Delta P_L$ is the system load variation magnitude (generally taken as 10% of maximum load), $B$ is the frequency bias coefficient, and $\Delta f_s$ is the allowable frequency variation magnitude.

variable_definitions.name: Required system regulating capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Power regulating amount that the AGC system needs to provide

variable_definitions.name: System load variation magnitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum fluctuation range of system load

variable_definitions.name: Frequency bias coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportional coefficient relating system frequency deviation to power

variable_definitions.name: Allowable frequency variation magnitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable deviation of system frequency

potential_questions: System maximum load 20000MW, B=800MW/0.1Hz, allowable frequency deviation ±0.1Hz, calculate required regulating capacity
task_description: 1. Determine system load variation magnitude \(\Delta P_L\) (typically 10% of maximum load). 2. Obtain frequency bias coefficient \(B\). 3. Determine allowable frequency variation magnitude \(\Delta f_s\). 4. Compute \(B\cdot\Delta f_s\). 5. Compute \(\Delta P_{\odot}=\Delta P_L-B\cdot\Delta f_s\).

full_context: Capacitor capacity calculation formula for n-th harmonic resonance: $ Q_{cx} = S_{d} \left( \frac{1}{n^{2}} - A \right) $. This formula is used to calculate the capacitor capacity that may trigger n-th harmonic resonance, where $S_d$ is the bus short-circuit capacity at the capacitor installation location, $n$ is the harmonic order, and $A$ is the ratio of per-phase inductive reactance to per-phase capacitive reactance of the capacitor installation.

variable_definitions.name: Resonant capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Critical capacity that may trigger n-th harmonic resonance

variable_definitions.name: Bus short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: System short-circuit capacity at the capacitor installation point

variable_definitions.name: Harmonic order
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of harmonic frequency to fundamental frequency

variable_definitions.name: Inductive-to-capacitive reactance ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of per-phase inductive reactance (X_L) to per-phase capacitive reactance (X_C) of the capacitor installation

potential_questions: Given Sd=2000MVA, n=5, A=0.06, calculate Qcx
task_description: 1. Obtain bus short-circuit capacity \(S_d\). 2. Determine harmonic order \(n\). 3. Obtain inductive-to-capacitive reactance ratio \(A\). 4. Compute \(1/n^2\). 5. Compute \((1/n^2-A)\). 6. Compute \(Q_{cx}=S_d\times(1/n^2-A)\).

full_context: Peak inrush current calculation formula when a single capacitor bank is switched into the grid: $ I_{y,\max} = \sqrt{2} I_{e} \left( 1 + \sqrt{\frac{X_{c}}{X_{L}'}} \right) $. This formula is used to calculate the maximum inrush current during capacitor-bank closing, where $I_e$ is the rated current of the capacitor bank, $X_c$ is the per-phase capacitive reactance of the capacitor bank, and $X_L'$ is the equivalent value combining the network inductive reactance and the series reactor inductive reactance.

variable_definitions.name: Peak inrush current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum transient inrush current at the instant the capacitor is switched in

variable_definitions.name: Rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Normal operating current of the capacitor bank

variable_definitions.name: Capacitive reactance of capacitor bank
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacitive component of the per-phase impedance of the capacitor bank

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent inductive reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent value of system inductive reactance and series reactor inductive reactance

potential_questions: Given Ie=0.8kA, Xc=40Ω, XL'=2Ω, calculate Iy,max
task_description: 1. Obtain capacitor bank rated current \(I_e\). 2. Calculate capacitive reactance \(X_c\). 3. Determine equivalent inductive reactance \(X_L'\). 4. Compute \(\sqrt{X_c/X_L'}\). 5. Compute \(1+\sqrt{X_c/X_L'}\). 6. Compute \(I_{y,\max}=\sqrt{2}\times I_e\times[1+\sqrt{X_c/X_L'}]\).

full_context: Steady-state voltage rise estimation formula caused by connecting a capacitor bank to the bus: $ \Delta U \approx U_{zM} \frac{Q_{c}}{S_{d}} $. This formula is used to estimate the bus voltage change after reactive power compensation equipment is connected, where $U_{zM}$ is the bus voltage before the capacitor bank is connected, $Q_c$ is the total capacity of the connected capacitor bank, and $S_d$ is the bus short-circuit capacity at the capacitor installation location.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage rise
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus voltage change caused by capacitor connection

variable_definitions.name: Original bus voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus voltage before the capacitor bank is connected

variable_definitions.name: Capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity of the capacitor bank connected to the bus

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: System short-circuit capacity at the installation point

potential_questions: Given UzM=110kV, Qc=50Mvar, Sd=2500MVA, calculate ΔU
task_description: 1. Measure original bus voltage \(U_{zM}\). 2. Determine capacitor bank capacity \(Q_c\). 3. Obtain installation-point short-circuit capacity \(S_d\). 4. Compute \(Q_c/S_d\). 5. Compute \(\Delta U=U_{zM}\times(Q_c/S_d)\).

full_context: Operating (pickup) value calculation formula for capacitor overcurrent protection: $ I_{dz} = \frac{K_k K_{jx} I_{e,\max}}{N_i} $. This formula is used to set the pickup current of capacitor protection devices, where $K_k$ is the reliability factor, $K_{jx}$ is the wiring factor, $I_{e,\max}$ is the long-term allowable maximum current of the capacitor bank, and $N_i$ is the current transformer ratio.

variable_definitions.name: Protection pickup current
variable_definitions.meaning: Current setting value that causes the protection device to operate

variable_definitions.name: Reliability factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Margin factor to ensure reliable protection operation

variable_definitions.name: Wiring factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for current transformer wiring configuration

variable_definitions.name: Maximum allowable current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum current allowed to pass through the capacitor bank continuously

variable_definitions.name: CT ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Current ratio of CT primary to secondary

potential_questions: Given Kk=1.2, Kjx=1.0, Ie,max=600A, Ni=600/5, calculate Idz
task_description: 1. Determine reliability factor \(K_k\). 2. Determine wiring factor \(K_{jx}\). 3. Obtain maximum allowable current \(I_{e,\max}\). 4. Determine CT ratio \(N_i\). 5. Compute \(I_{dz}=(K_k\times K_{jx}\times I_{e,\max})/N_i\).

full_context: Amplitude formula for the n-th harmonic current generated by a thyristor-controlled reactor (TCR): $ I_{nm} = \frac{U_m}{\pi X} \times \frac{4}{n(n^2 - 1)} (\cos \alpha \sin n\alpha - n \sin \alpha \cos n\alpha) $. This formula is used to calculate the characteristic harmonic current produced by a TCR at a given conduction angle, where $U_m$ is the phase-voltage peak value, $X$ is the fundamental-frequency reactance of the reactor, $\alpha$ is the firing delay angle, and $n$ is the harmonic order (3, 5, 7, ...).

variable_definitions.name: Harmonic current amplitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplitude of the n-th harmonic current

variable_definitions.name: Phase-voltage peak value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum value of the system phase voltage

variable_definitions.name: Fundamental reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Inductive reactance of the reactor at fundamental frequency

variable_definitions.name: Firing delay angle
variable_definitions.meaning: Phase angle corresponding to the thyristor firing instant

variable_definitions.name: Harmonic order
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of harmonic frequency to fundamental frequency

potential_questions: Given Um=100kV, X=50Ω, α=120°, n=5, calculate Inm
task_description: 1. Obtain phase-voltage peak value \(U_m\). 2. Determine fundamental reactance \(X\). 3. Determine firing delay angle \(\alpha\). 4. Specify harmonic order \(n\). 5. Compute trigonometric term \((\cos\alpha\,\sin(n\alpha)-n\sin\alpha\,\cos(n\alpha))\). 6. Compute coefficient \(4/[n(n^2-1)]\). 7. Compute \(I_{nm}=(U_m/(\pi X))\times[4/(n(n^2-1))]\times(\cos\alpha\,\sin(n\alpha)-n\sin\alpha\,\cos(n\alpha))\).

full_context: The longitudinal electromotive force induced by magnetic induction on a telecommunication line should be calculated by Eq. (5.2.1): $E_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega M_i l_{pi} I_s k_{50}$. This formula is used to calculate the total longitudinal induced EMF on a telecommunication line under transmission-line fault conditions. It is applicable to protection design against hazardous influence of 35kV and above three-phase AC overhead transmission lines on telecommunication lines. In calculation, the near-parallel section should be segmented, and the shielding effect of grounding conductors should be considered.

variable_definitions.name: Longitudinal induced EMF (magnetic induction)
variable_definitions.meaning: Total potential difference induced on a telecommunication line by transmission-line fault current

variable_definitions.name: Angular frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Angular frequency of transmission-line current, \omega=2\pi f

variable_definitions.name: Mutual inductance coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Mutual inductance coefficient between the i-th segment of the transmission line and the telecommunication line

variable_definitions.name: Length of approach segment
variable_definitions.meaning: Length over which the i-th segment of the transmission line runs approximately parallel and close to the telecommunication line

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: Fault current for single-phase-to-ground or two-phase-to-ground short circuit on the transmission line

variable_definitions.name: Comprehensive electromagnetic shielding factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Total shielding effect factor of grounding conductors within the approach segment at 50Hz

potential_questions: Given M_i=0.5 H/km, l_{pi}=2km, I_s=10kA, k_{50}=0.8 (3 segments total), find E_s
task_description: 1. Determine number of segments \(n\). 2. Obtain \(M_i\) for each segment. 3. Obtain \(l_{pi}\) for each segment. 4. Compute \(\omega=2\pi\times 50\). 5. Compute each segment term \(\omega M_i l_{pi} I_s k_{50}\). 6. Sum all segment terms to obtain \(E_s\). 7. Output total \(E_s\).

formula: U_k = \frac{E_1 l_{TBk} + E_2 l_{TAk}}{l_{TAk} + l_{TBk}} + \frac{\pm U_B l_{TAk} \mp U_A l_{TBk}}{l_{TAk} + l_{TBk}}
full_context: When the telecommunication line is grounded at both ends (i.e., arresters at both ends operate), the telecommunication line-to-ground voltage corresponding to transmission-line fault point k is calculated by Eq. (5.2.13-6): $U_k = \frac{E_1 l_{TBk} + E_2 l_{TAk}}{l_{TAk} + l_{TBk}} + \frac{\pm U_B l_{TAk} \mp U_A l_{TBk}}{l_{TAk} + l_{TBk}}$. This formula is used to determine the line-to-ground voltage on the telecommunication line at the location corresponding to the transmission-line fault point, applicable to hazardous-influence checks when terminal arresters operate. When E_1 > E_2, take U_A as negative; when E_1 < E_2, take U_B as negative.

variable_definitions.name: Magnetically induced line-to-ground voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Line-to-ground potential of the telecommunication line at fault point k

variable_definitions.name: Longitudinal induced EMF (magnetic induction)
variable_definitions.meaning: Induced EMF on segments l_{TAk} and l_{TBk}

variable_definitions.name: Distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Distances from the point corresponding to fault point k to the two terminal offices along the telecommunication line

variable_definitions.name: Grounding resistance voltage drop
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage drops across the grounding resistances of terminal arresters

potential_questions: Given E_1=500V, E_2=300V, l_{TAk}=2km, l_{TBk}=3km, U_A=50V, U_B=40V (E_1>E_2), find U_k
task_description: 1. Determine the relationship between \(E_1\) and \(E_2\). 2. Select the sign of \(U_A\)/\(U_B\) according to the rule. 3. Compute numerator \(E_1 l_{TBk}+E_2 l_{TAk}\). 4. Compute denominator \(l_{TAk}+l_{TBk}\). 5. Compute the first fraction term. 6. Compute the second fraction term (including sign adjustment). 7. Sum to obtain \(U_k\).

formula: I_b = 18 \frac{m'}{m + m' + 2} f U_N \left[ \sum n(a) L_T k_d k_{s1} k_{s2} + \sum \frac{N(a_B) \pm N(a_A)}{a_B \pm a_A} L_T k_d k_{s1} k_{s2} \right] \times 10^{-6}
full_context: When a single-phase-to-ground short circuit occurs in a system with neutral point not directly grounded, the current through the human body caused by capacitive coupling on a nearby overhead open-wire telecommunication line should be calculated by Eq. (5.3.1): $I_b = 18 \frac{m'}{m + m' + 2} f U_N \left[ \sum n(a) L_T k_d k_{s1} k_{s2} + \sum \frac{N(a_B) \pm N(a_A)}{a_B \pm a_A} L_T k_d k_{s1} k_{s2} \right] \times 10^{-6}$. The formula is used to assess electric-shock risk to maintenance personnel touching an insulated telecommunication wire under single-phase ground-fault conditions. Sign rule for the second term: use “−” for oblique approach, and “+” for crossing span.

variable_definitions.name: Human body current
variable_definitions.meaning: Current through the human body caused by capacitive coupling

variable_definitions.name: Number of contacted wires
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of earth-insulated telecommunication conductors touched by a person (e.g., for a two-wire circuit, m'=2)

variable_definitions.name: Number of grounded wires
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of grounded conductors in the telecommunication line (e.g., for single-wire telephone, m=1)

variable_definitions.name: Frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage frequency of the transmission line

variable_definitions.name: Rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of the transmission line

variable_definitions.name: Coupling coefficient functions
variable_definitions.meaning: Coupling functions related to approach distance, determined from Table 5.3.1

variable_definitions.name: Telecommunication line length
variable_definitions.meaning: Total length of the telecommunication line in the calculated section

variable_definitions.name: Ground-wire shielding factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Shielding factor of the transmission line ground wire (k_d=0.75 when ground wire exists)

variable_definitions.name: Tree shielding factors
variable_definitions.meaning: Tree shielding factors on the transmission-line side (k_{s1}) and telecommunication-line side (k_{s2})

variable_definitions.name: Approach distances
variable_definitions.meaning: Distances at the two ends of an oblique approach or crossing span section

potential_questions: Given two-wire circuit (m'=2,m=0), f=50Hz, U_N=110kV, L_T=5km, k_d=0.75, k_{s1}=k_{s2}=0.7, parallel section n(a)=0.1, crossing section a_A=10m, a_B=20m, N(a)=0.05 from table, find I_b
task_description: 1. Determine \(m'\) and \(m\). 2. Compute factor \(m'/(m+m'+2)\). 3. Compute \(fU_N\). 4. Compute the sum for parallel sections: \(n(a)L_T k_d k_{s1} k_{s2}\). 5. Compute the sum for crossing/oblique sections: \(\frac{N(a_B)\pm N(a_A)}{a_B\pm a_A}\cdot L_T k_d k_{s1} k_{s2}\). 6. Add the two summation terms. 7. Multiply by the coefficient and \(10^{-6}\) to obtain \(I_b\).

full_context: For a solidly grounded neutral system, the total noise-meter electromotive force for interference impact of a transmission line on an audio-frequency two-wire telephone circuit should be calculated by Eq. (6.2.1): $e = \sqrt{e_{bv}^2 + e_{bI}^2 + e_{rI}^2}$. This formula combines noise induced by the voltage balanced component, current balanced component, and current residual component. It is applicable for evaluating the interference level on telephone circuits under normal operation of transmission lines. Loop effects are neglected and only unbalance influence components are considered.

variable_definitions.name: Total noise-meter EMF
variable_definitions.meaning: Total weighted noise voltage induced in the telephone circuit

variable_definitions.name: Noise from voltage balanced component
variable_definitions.meaning: Unbalance influence component induced by the balanced voltage component of the transmission line

variable_definitions.name: Noise from current balanced component
variable_definitions.meaning: Unbalance influence component induced by the balanced current component of the transmission line

variable_definitions.name: Noise from current residual component
variable_definitions.meaning: Unbalance influence component induced by the residual current component of the transmission line

potential_questions: Given e_{bv}=1.2mV, e_{bI}=0.8mV, e_{rI}=1.5mV, find total noise e
task_description: 1. Obtain \(e_{bv}\). 2. Obtain \(e_{bI}\). 3. Obtain \(e_{rI}\). 4. Compute \(e_{bv}^2\). 5. Compute \(e_{bI}^2\). 6. Compute \(e_{rI}^2\). 7. Sum the three squared terms. 8. Take square root to get \(e\).

full_context: The adjustable output voltage range of a dedicated DC power supply system for communications is -43V to -58V, and the voltage variation range at the receiving terminals of communication equipment is -40V to -57V. This requirement ensures reliable operation of communication equipment within the standard voltage range and is applicable to design acceptance of in-plant communication power supply systems in thermal power plants.

variable_definitions.name: Output voltage range
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable range of DC output voltage of the communication power supply system

potential_questions: If measured receiving-terminal voltage is -45V, is it compliant?
task_description: 1. Measure power supply output voltage \(U_{\text{out}}\). 2. Verify \(U_{\text{out}}\in[-58\,\mathrm{V},-43\,\mathrm{V}]\). 3. Measure equipment terminal voltage \(U_{\text{term}}\). 4. Verify \(U_{\text{term}}\in[-57\,\mathrm{V},-40\,\mathrm{V}]\).
